data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Past The Rerating, Entering A Durable Income Phase
data.description:
Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) may have exhausted some of the easy rerating phase, but its cash flow strength and declining downside risks still augur well for income investors. After a multi-year capital intensive building phase, EPD has now entered a higher cash flow regime - primarily supported by improving EBITDA, lower growth capex, and fully contracted export assets.

Distributions appear well covered, and leverage is more comfortable than earlier regimes. Excess cash should further support deleveraging as well as buybacks. That means the forward outlook for EPD should be anchored in strong income and supportive buyback accretion. Rerating opportunities due to the macro environment (like an entry in 2022-23) or the companies own triggers driven by lower leverage and better income predictability, are limited. I see a high single-digit to low double-digit total return possibility for fresh capital. Hence, EPD is still a Buy - not its greatest phase, but potentially a great regime for income investors over total return use cases.

Structural Advantages and EPD Differentiation

EPD's business model is relatively agnostic to commodity prices - about 90% of its natural gas processing contracts have fee components. That is, cash flow depends on usage. EPD's vertical integration structure also stands apart. It does not own just pipelines. It also owns processing plants, fractionators, storage facilities, and export terminals. This diversifies cash flow generation avenues.

EPD is a major exporter of LPG and ethane (~15% of global LPG supply). Hence, its earnings depend more on exports, retaining earning capabilities even if domestic demand slows. The reliability of distributable cash flow (DCF) coverage is seen across stress regimes, including oil crashes. The coverage ratio has only improved further since 2019-20.

Since around 2020, EPD has consistently reduced leverage from over ~4x to ~3.3x now. This is a regime when EPD has moved to self-funded capex, emphasized buybacks, and slowed distributions as a % of DCF, while still retaining high yields. This regime has made EPD an income-first product with a greater degree of reliability.

Past data clearly show how distributions have been relatively protected despite shocks to share prices. The cash flow per unit has remained relatively resilient. Shocks have often actually made it a great time to load up more, as yields usually mechanically spike in such regimes, leading to sharper recoveries. This flywheel mechanism, driven by income interest, has meant the floor is usually higher and temporary.

Rolling Returns Analysis - Where are We

An analysis of the historical rolling return data (3-year periods) should help establish the mechanics of share price and total return expectations. I assume the current yields of ~6-7% are backed by more than sufficient distributable cash. But the share prices are what have varied in the past and are the key determinants of both risks and upside.

In the 2016-2019 era of growth-first MLPs, the share prices have seen phases of pressure (negative 3-year returns). Towards the end of this phase, some stability returned to share prices, but overall income did most of the heavy lifting. Interestingly, the DCF coverage charts show this era also saw thinner DCF coverage as growth capex dominated cash flow use. This meant EPD saw more skepticism from income investors, adding to leverage and equity funding risks, keeping share prices and valuations compressed.

The 2020-21 period was one that initially saw liquidity-led panic selling. However, as the income strength became obvious, investor interest rebounded. From here till 2023, rerating picked up steam and price returns turned meaningfully positive - this rerating coincides with a reduction in leverage and a self-funded capex regime. The 2024-25 period saw peak 3-year total returns over 120% - partly helped by entry timings post-COVID lows and also helped by EPD's transition from a growth MLP to an income-focused, deleveraged one. That double support that EPD benefited from, for investors who entered around the 2020-21 lows, is not expected going ahead. Entering at lows is no longer a case for fresh capital, and rerating triggers are now already apparent (leverage and income stability).

Forward Looking Analysis

The forward return outlook depends on three critical variables, going by how EPD's historical total returns have been shaped. The most important variable in my view, is distribution coverage and growth. EPD has moved past a survival regime here to surplus with far higher coverage today. For entries today, assuming marginal growth (because even surplus distributable cash allows for it), investors can anchor expectations at around 8-9% on a cost basis (a couple of percentage points higher than today's yields).

In fact, even if yields remain in the high single-digit zone, the coverage is likely to get better with capex falling further and EBITDA starting to show investment returns. According to management commentary during the Q3 FY2025 earnings call,

we expect an inflection point in discretionary free cash flow in 2026 as we have completed a 4-year period of large investments, both organic and acquisitions that enhanced our -- have enhanced and expanded our integrated footprint in the Permian and Haynesville basins and our premium -- premier wellhead to market businesses serving domestic as well as international markets via our marine terminals.

The coverage does not only look better, but more durable too. There is a lot of volume stability due to PDP durability - less slowdown effects when drilling slows. This expectation is already evidenced -

We have a producer in Midland that finished their development program a year ago. Today, in Midland, those volumes are flat with where they were then.

Buybacks have been positioned as programmatic, and therefore predictable, increasing DCF per unit. Additionally, the drawdown risks appear even lower looking at the 90% contracted levels in LPG and fully contracted ethane business today.

The remaining variables impact share price outcomes. Balance sheet and Leverage (the second variable in my list that has moved share prices in the past) improvements as a catalyst are past their peak effects as the new regime is established. However, there is room for improvement in leverage numbers in 2026 as Bahia, Frac 14 and Neches River EBITDA flow in. While this is a mechanical impact of growth investments already made (hence not a surprise), it should help valuation pressures in 2026, even if it stops being a rerating catalyst any longer.

The third variable also affects share price outlook, and adds optionality - growth. This lever is applicable to EPD (instead of the broader MLP segment) because of its export-facing business structure. I see reasonable optionality in growth in export volumes (not fully linked to domestic macro). EPD should see decent fee growth on throughput, even if prices fluctuate. Ethane exports are already showing strong growth, with latent supply excess (no need for new drilling in the near term). As demand grows, this alone could help exports significantly. Additionally, when prices are weak, storage and contango mechanics should help.

The U.S. will export, what's needed to balance the market and price will ultimately adjust upon that global demand. So we're not necessarily worried about demand.

Great Risk-Adjusted Returns Potential

Overall, EPD's thesis looks robust, and conservatively, should see 8-9% from yields, decent support from a stable balance sheet and leverage, and high probability growth optionality that could easily add another couple of percentage points to total returns. That is where my high single-digit to low double-digit return expectations stem from.

Importantly, the risks are relatively lower too, as EPD's total return outlook appears to be driven more by per-unit cash generation abilities than rates. We are in a rate normalization regime there too, and even if it stays higher for longer, I do not see valuation compression - I see low room for valuation upside at worst (something I have already assumed conservatively).

The only remaining risks that I will monitor in 2026 are related to project execution risks that could impact expected EBITDA ramp-up. Regulatory risks in exports are also baked in - export growth is an optionality in my mind. It is also more dependent on price clearing mechanisms than discretionary demand. Another risk is a prolonged slowdown in Permian or Haynesville drilling. This could lead to slower distribution growth, but I do not see base volume impacts. PDP volume durability should hold up.

Overall, EPD is a Strong Buy for an income investor, despite this not being the period of maximum total return potential. This is still the best time for income investors seeking great risk-adjusted total returns, though.

data.title: First Guaranty Bancshares Illustrates The Risks Of Investing In Micro-Cap Banks
data.description:
I have written about a number of small-cap and micro-cap banks over the last year. In many cases, the gains that are achieved by investing in their stocks are well above the average for both the overall market and the financial sector. However, these smaller regional and community banks bring added risks. They do not have the financial cushion that larger banks have when problems do arise, and just a few loan defaults can cause significant problems on their balance sheets.

First Guaranty Bancshares (FGBI) is an example of the losses that investors may face with this type of investment. Its stock is down over 25% in the last 12 months as the company deals with a number of issues with its loan portfolio. This is not a one-time problem for the company, as FGBI has lost 52% of its value over the last five years. As I write this analysis, First Guaranty is the lowest-rated regional bank rated by Seeking Alpha, with a Quant score of 1.30.

As of January 14, FGBI has a market cap of just $117.0 million, about a third of what it was in June of 2022. The company made a number of moves in Q2 and Q3 to attempt to “stop the bleeding,” but only time will tell if the bank can recover to its previous levels of value. In FY 2022, First Guaranty earned $2.47 per share. In Q3 2025, the company lost almost that much in one three-month period.

Even if all goes as planned, it will take some time for the company to return to its former profit levels. However, recent insider trading activity points to signs that all hope is not lost, at least within the company. Until First Guaranty can report a clean quarter to give greater visibility of its future earning potential, I see its stock as no better than a Hold, and that would be for investors who are willing to stomach potential additional bad news in the near future. As I will address at the end of my analysis, there is still a good bit of uncertainty surrounding FGBI's Q4 2025 financial report that will be released on January 30.

Company Overview

First Guaranty Bancshares serves as the holding company for First Guaranty Bank. The company has a history that dates back to 1934 and is headquartered in Hammond, LA. Its banking operations have a rather unique footprint, with the majority of its 31 branches located in Louisiana and Texas. The company does have one additional branch in Northern Kentucky and another one in Bridgeport, WV. Those two branches were converted from loan production offices in 2022.

First Guaranty reported total assets of $3.80 billion at the end of Q3 2025, 4.4% less than it had at the start of this year. Included in those assets are an investment securities portfolio worth $697 million and loans valued at $2.28 billion before allowances for credit losses. The company also held a cash position of $754 million as of September 30, 2025.

The majority of the bank’s loan mix is allocated to some type of real estate. Commercial real estate comprises 43.9% of the portfolio. Residential mortgages take up the next largest share at 19.3%. Construction and land development loans are the only other type with a double-digit allocation at 10.1%. A full list of the types of loans issued by the bank and their totals from the last four reported quarters is provided below.

The bank held $3.35 billion in deposits at the end of Q3. Only 11.8% of those were held in non-interest-bearing accounts, which increases FGBI’s cost of deposits significantly. About $1.35 billion, or 40.3%, is time deposits, which pay an above-average interest rate. This leaves another issue for the bank to work through in future quarters.

First Guaranty Bancshares does pay a dividend, but the payout is only $0.01 per quarter. The company cut its dividend twice in 2024, a sign of the troubles brewing in its operations. As recently as Q2 2024, FGBI paid a quarterly distribution of $0.16, a rate that it had held consistent since Q2 2007.

Analysis of First Guaranty Bancshares

The last two quarterly reports for FGBI have been so dreadful that it is hard for me to know where to start to make my analysis. In Q3 2025, the company announced that the bankruptcy of an auto parts manufacturer had caused it to record a provision for credit losses of almost $48 million, with $39.8 million connected to this one borrower. The company also moved to eliminate $12.9 million in goodwill on its balance sheet, wiping out almost 82% of its reported intangible assets. The result was a loss of $45 million in the quarter, or $3.01 per share, bringing the loss for FY 2025 to $58.5 million, or $4.45 per share. As a comparison, in the first nine months of 2024, FGBI reported positive earnings of $11.4 million, or $0.78 per share.

This third quarter from last year followed a second quarter that was also filled with a number of problems. The company charged off $1.1 million in loan balances in Q2 and issued new stock that diluted current shareholders by over 20%. The biggest culprit for this dilution was the issuance of 1.98 million shares to a company director in exchange for the forgiveness of $15 million in subordinated debt.

As part of its most recent quarterly report, FGBI reported some of the worst loan quality metrics that I have seen in any of my reviews for Seeking Alpha. They were bad at the end of last year, and they are continuing to move in the wrong direction. Non-performing assets represent over 5.5% of the bank’s total loans. The ratio of non-performing loans to total loans was just over 5%. The bank’s net charge-offs to average loans stood at 1.55%, and there are signs that the bank may still have to allocate more resources to cover ongoing issues. Its allowance for future credit losses, while much greater than it was in Q2 2025, is still below 100% at 75.01%.

The results from the last few years at the bank have significantly reduced its tangible book value. The greatest declines have come in the last 12 months. At the end of 2022, the TBV for the company was $17.23 per share. At the end of 2024, this value had dipped to $16.48. After the last quarterly report from First Guaranty, it stood at $12.08.

Additional Risks of Troubled Loans

The issues with bad loans at First Guaranty Bank will likely linger for an extended period of time. There is a good chance that the bank will have to make additional considerations for non-performing loans in the near future. I have included a chart that lists the 10 largest non-performing assets for FGBI that the company provided in its most recent 10-Q filing. These loans have a total value of $96.9 million. The top loan relationship on the list is connected to an independent living center in Louisiana that has already been charged down by 34.2%. The second one on the list, described as a commercial lease, is related to the auto parts bankruptcy.

To take this one step further, I have included a snapshot of the bank’s top 10 substandard relationships, which have a total value of $220.7 million. The top concern on the list is directly related to the auto parts manufacturer. The principal balances of $52 million consist of the $17.2 million lease mentioned earlier, which was in nonaccrual status at the end of Q3. It also includes three additional leases totaling $34.8 million that were on accrual status at the time the most recent update was given. The moral of the story here is that there are potentially more problems in the pipeline for FGBI.

It should also be noted that insider buying does not guarantee future positive results. There were three insider purchases worth a total of $1.6 million made two days after the end of Q3 2025. The company would release its Q3 quarterly report full of additional bad news 29 days later. There is a chance that more disappointing news will be released as part of the company's Q4 2025 results.

Finding Reasons for Optimism

Finding any good news for FGBI is difficult, but if the bank can get past its loan issues, and that is a big if, it has made decisions in other parts of its operations that could make it a little easier to return to profitability. For example, the bank has been able to lower its non-interest expenses thanks to a sizeable reduction in labor force in the last 12 months. In Q3 2024, FGBI had 404 full-time employees. One year later, in Q3 2025, the number was just 339, a headcount decrease of 16.1%. When the goodwill charge is removed from the Q3 results, non-interest expenses for the company totaled $17.3 million, a $600,000 reduction from the end of 2024 and $2.4 million less than Q3 2024.

The decline in tangible book value for the company mentioned earlier has corresponded with FGBI's reduction in share price. As of January 14, FGBI is trading at 0.63 times its TBV. This, of course, is a reflection of the market’s opinion of the bank’s current operations and prospects for the future. However, if the company can right the ship, it could also be a sign of deep value.

One additional potentially good sign to consider is the insider buying that has taken place over the last six months. As recently as January 2, the activity surrounding the company’s stock from insiders has appeared quite positive. The same director who was involved in the debt-for-equity swap in Q2 2025 is the one who has made the largest additional purchases over the last four months. There are a number of reasons why insiders sell their stock, but there is really only one reason they buy it. The market has already recognized what this activity may signal. FGBI has gained 41.5% since the close of trading on January 2.

Q4 2025 Earnings Expectations

When the quarterly results for Q4 2025 are released on January 30, it will be interesting to see just how much the company will be able to show a return to normal. From my perspective, the bank may need to increase its allowance for loan losses further, which could impact bottom-line results in the short term. However, the company may have some good news to report as well. As part of its earlier financial reports, FGBI stated that its largest OREO property, a $7.4 million land development loan that originated in Texas, was under agreement for sale. That transaction was expected to close in Q4 2025.

It will also be important to see if the company has been able to reduce more risk from its loan portfolio. Total loans for the bank have decreased 16% since the end of 2024. Concentration risk has been lessened by cutting back on commercial real estate loans. The bank did hire a new Chief Lending Officer in December. Perhaps this can also signal improvement in its lending standards moving forward. FGBI's risk-weighted capital ratio did improve, year-over-year, in Q3 2025 to 12.34% from 11.66% in Q3 2024. Seeing a continuation of that trend would be important for investor confidence as well.

Earnings estimates still expect the bank to lose $0.29 per share in Q4. However, this is a company that earned as much as $0.70 per share as recently as Q2 and Q3 in 2022. The one analyst who covers FGBI has projected that the company will not return to profitability until Q1 2026. The estimate for FY 2026 earnings shows a gain of $0.38 per share.

The Bottom Line

The overall picture presented by First Guaranty Bancshares is not a pretty one. Two things keep me from moving my opinion on the stock to a sell. The first is its already depressed valuation. I like the fact that company management appeared to treat Q3 2025 as a full cleansing, eliminating its goodwill while also addressing its problem loans. The second reason for a sliver of optimism is the recent insider activity. The three purchases made on January 2 totaled over $1.8 million in value. These things are not enough to make the stock a Buy by any means, but with insiders putting so much skin in the game, the stock may be worth a Hold for investors with the patience and a high tolerance for near-term volatility.

data.title: CGO: Deep Discounts Present An Opportunity To Accumulate
data.description:
Overview

With market indices trading near all-time highs, this has created uncertainty around whether the positive momentum can continue. As a result, some investors may want to allocate capital towards global funds that may have more reasonable valuations. This is where the Calamos Global Total Return Fund (CGO) can serve as a valuable choice for investors seeking diversity and income generation that reduces reliance on U.S. assets. When I previously covered CGO, I issued a buy rating due to the discounted valuation. Since my last coverage, CGO has released an updated annual report for 2025, so I wanted to reassess its value proposition now that we've crossed into the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CGO's share price has increased by 5.1%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to partially participate in a positive market momentum of the market. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 14.4% over the same time frame. CGO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 8% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. This makes the fund a great option for investors that want to diversify their income away from dependence on U.S. securities.

Surprisingly, CGO still trades at a deeply discounted valuation, and this can be a great time for long-term investors to accumulate. However, the fund has some structural vulnerabilities that need to be improved. For instance, the reporting indicates that the fund is entirely reliant on positive net realized capital gains to fuel its NAV growth and support distributions. As a fund that prioritizes income generation, I would like to see its net investment income levels improve going forward. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that CGO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, CGO has total managed assets of $178M that are spread across a diverse range of assets. The fund emphasizes investments on a global scale while also prioritizing sustainable monthly distributions. The fund has a listed gross expense ratio of 2.38%, but this is a bit misleading because it includes other fees related to leverage and operating costs. When excluding these costs, the actual management fee sits at 1% of managed assets. The fund takes a blended approach with its portfolio, and the breakdown by asset class is as follows:

  • Common Stock: 62.6%
  • Convertibles: 20.5%
  • Corporate Bonds: 7.5%
  • Cash and Receivables/Payables: 4.1%
  • Other: 2.8%
  • Bank Loans: 0.9%
  • Sovereign Bonds: 0.8%
  • Options: 0.7%

This blending approach allows CGO to capture both growth and income during shifting market cycles. Furthermore, CGO maintains an efficient level of diversity across sectors. As we can see below, CGO is most heavily allocated to Information Technology companies at 22% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Industrials and Financials investments, which accounts for 14.5% and 14.1% of assets, respectively.

When looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that CGO provides exposure to many high-quality companies. For instance, the largest position is in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) at 4.6% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable companies such as Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOG), The Boeing Company (BA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN), just to name a few.

As the fund's name implies, CGO also provides exposure to assets on a global scale. Despite CGO being most heavily allocated to North American securities, the fund still provides a well-balanced mix across the globe. This can be a great approach for investors that don't want to go all in on a global fund that eliminates U.S. exposure. The portfolio spreads its assets across different regions of Asia, Europe, Latin America, Africa, and the Caribbean.

Valuation & Performance Risks

At the time of my last coverage, CGO traded at a discount to NAV of 4.2%. Despite the fund's share price trending higher over the last year, the discount to NAV has actually grown wider. CGO now trades at a discount to NAV of 9.13%, which indicates that investors may be able to take advantage of this discount opportunity. For reference, CGO has traded at an average discount to NAV of 2.35% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that CGO now trades at one of the deepest discount to NAV levels over the last decade. Therefore, I believe that this is a great time to accumulate shares if you are a long-term buy-and-hold investor.

Since my last coverage, CGO has also released an updated annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to a loss of $0.13 per share, while net realized gains landed at $2.40 per share. Looking back over prior years, we can see that CGO has consistently experienced losses with its net investment income. This is an indication that the income received from the fund cannot outpace the underlying operating expenses. This can also be an indication that CGO is being negatively impacted by the higher interest rate environment since the cost of its leverage may be high.

Since net investment income is consistently negative, the fund is entirely reliant on net realized gains to fuel its NAV growth and support its distributions over time. The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions, so it can be hard to get a sense of how well the fund will do during any given year. While this reliance may stunt growth potential, CGO continues to demonstrate that it can grow its NAV during positive market momentum. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $15.82 per share
  • 2022: $9.59 per share
  • 2023: $9.12 per share
  • 2024: $11.80 per share
  • 2025: $13.11 per share

Since CGO needs to actively sell off portions of its upside growth, investors should limit their expectations around its ability to provide meaningful upside price growth. Additionally, investors must remember that a large portion of its portfolio is structured as income instruments. When measuring the performance over the last ten years, we can see how CGO's share price has increased by a little more than 12%, despite market indices running to all-time highs. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 179% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a consistent rate of high distributions over time.

As part of the fund's operating strategy, CGO utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 31.75% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $77M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment, and it can increase the overall risk profile of the fund. Although the Fed has initiated three separate 25 basis point cuts to interest rates throughout 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. A higher interest rate can directly translate to a higher cost of debt, which negatively impacts the operating spread between its cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities.

As a result, we can see that CGO's share price has shared an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate. I believe that CGO's share price can recover in a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months. As we can see below, the fund's share price has struggled to maintain enough momentum to get back to its pre-pandemic levels. So as interest rates decline, the fund's operating spread may improve, and a lower cost of debt can be a positive growth catalyst for the fund's holdings.

Additionally, investors need to understand the tradeoff of focusing on income generation. If you are an investor who wants to achieve the highest possible total return, the focus on income may be the wrong approach because a high dividend yield doesn't automatically result in a high total return. Since the fund has to actively sell off portions of its upside growth, CGO may not be able to fully participate in the growth of markets. For instance, we can see how CGO has underperformed the more traditional iShares Global 100 ETF (IOO) over the last ten-year period.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8%. Since the fund's underlying earnings have been impacted by the higher interest rate environment, payouts were reduced in mid-2022 to reflect the uncertainty of the market at the time. However, CGO has failed to raise the dividend back to its prior level since then. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.96 per share. Referring to the annual report, we can see that CGO has a mixed ability at generating income that can outpace distributions based on market conditions. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2021: $4.99 per share
  • 2022: ($5.08) per share
  • 2023: $0.49 per share
  • 2024: $3.64 per share
  • 2025: $2.27 per share

As we can see, the earnings can wildly vary due to the fact that capital gains make up the entirety of each period. In a perfect world, I would like to see net investment income substantially improve to reduce vulnerability to market conditions. On a positive note, I do like the fact that CGO has been able to generate earnings that can support multiple years worth of payouts. This instills confidence that the distributions can be sustained.

2025's earnings of $2.27 per share / $0.96 annual payout = 2.36 years.

Lastly, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that all the payouts on a YTD basis were fueled by short-term capital gains. Short-term capital gains are taxed at the highest rates for investors. Therefore, distributions may not offer any tax efficiency for income investors. A position in CGO would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on CGO at this time due to the deeply discounted valuation. The discount to NAV pricing can indicate that there's a disconnect in the fund's share price, and CGO may be due for some upside growth. Although the fund is reliant on net realized gains to support distributions, CGO has demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that can support multiple years worth of payouts during a single period. Therefore, CGO continues to be a great option for investors that seek a reliable source of income generation. However, it's important to note that the distributions may not be tax-efficient outside tax-advantaged accounts.

data.title: Bank Of Korea Stands Pat, Ending Its Easing Cycle, Amid Currency Concerns
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

The Bank of Korea remains on hold, with unanimous decision

By standing pat on Thursday, the Bank of Korea made clear it’s prioritising stabilising financial markets and improving financial instability. Despite a more optimistic view on the growth outlook, the BoK remains cautious about risks in the housing market, household debt, and FX volatility. The BoK assessed that inflation will remain broadly in line with current projections, although there are upside risks from a weakening currency. These factors justified today's no-action decision and the shift in the forward guidance toward a neutral stance.

The BoK signalled this is the end of its easing cycle in various ways. The three-month forward guidance shows fewer members expect a rate cut than last time, shifting from a 3-3 split in November to 1-5 now. One member argued that domestic growth remained sluggish thus preferred to keep the possibility of a rate cut. However, most board members maintained a cautious stance given recent KRW and housing market trends. In addition, the BoK removed the “leave room for potential rate cut” phrase from its statement. At the press conference, Governor Rhee emphasised that the greater policy focus is on financial instability and FX volatility.

The shift was faster than expected, but this shouldn’t be interpreted as a move toward rate hikes anytime soon. As noted in our recent report, a K-shaped recovery will likely constrain monetary policy decisions despite expectations that overall growth will accelerate. We forecast 2.0% GDP growth in 2026, which is higher than the BoK’s current 1.8% outlook. We had argued that the negative output gap would narrow by year-end, reducing the need for more easing. Even if growth recovers faster than expected, a fragile recovery outside of the semiconductor sector still requires supportive macro policies. Fiscal policy will play a critical role, while monetary policy is likely to offer targeted liquidity through lending facilities. Today, the BoK extended the Temporary Special Support program for low-credit self-employed and for SMEs by six months.

Considering recent macro conditions and the outlook, we maintain our view that the BoK will keep its policy rate at 2.5% throughout 2026.

FX was major factor in today’s decision

During the press conference, Governor Rhee allocated considerable time explaining recent KRW movements. He provided justification for the interventions conducted in December, noting that KRW volatility was excessive and not aligned with fundamental market conditions. He also assessed that renewed won weakness in January was mainly due to global dollar strength, while strong USD demand from domestic investors also contributed. Even as the National Pension Service (NPS) and exporters increased FX hedging and dollar supply over the past month, retail investors continued to expand overseas investments.

Recent statistics showed that onshore investors' net purchase of US equities totalled around $2bln as of 13 January, surpassing December's figure of $1.8bln. Following US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's comments warning of excessive KRW movements, the USDKRW gained up to 1%, rising from 1,475 to 1,460. However, this morning, it rose back to 1,472 level.

Retail investors contributed recent KRW weakness

We anticipate the USDKRW may reach the 1,500 level in the near term, then moderate to 1,450 by mid-year. Strategic hedging by NPS is expected to become influential while the government will follow up FX stabilisation measures announced in December. The Reshoring Investment Account is likely to play a meaningful role by incentivising the repatriation of dollars into the domestic equity market. For investments up to 50 million KRW, investors who sell overseas stocks and purchase domestic stocks will benefit from reduced or fully exempted capital gains tax on their overseas holdings. These products are expected to launch by the end of January or February. Retail investors' capital flows are deemed the key determinant of short-term won trends, and this initiative could help slow the pace of outward investment.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: 2025 - A Pivotal, Resurgent Year For U.S. Equities
data.description:
For the third consecutive year, the U.S. equity new issuance market extended its upward trajectory, navigating several periods of heightened volatility along the way. The year opened amid the inauguration of a new U.S. presidential administration, elevated geopolitical tensions, and persistently high inflation—factors that combined to create a complex and uncertain backdrop for both issuers and investors.

Activity in January and February carried forward the momentum that closed out 2024, with 76 and 77 deals priced, respectively. Sentiment, however, deteriorated in March following the announcement of higher U.S. tariffs on key global trading partners, slowing issuance to 64 deals. As a result, first-quarter volume totaled 217 deals—down 13% from the fourth quarter of 2024 and 11.8% below the year-earlier period. The deceleration carried into April, when just 53 deals were priced, tying August 2024 for the lowest monthly total since October 2023. Conditions improved in May and June as trade tensions eased and volatility receded, allowing issuance to rebound sharply. For the first time since the record-setting year of 2021, monthly deal counts reached triple digits in both months, driving second-quarter issuance to 284 deals—the highest quarterly total since Q4 2021.

Momentum carried into the second half of the year, supported by strong corporate earnings and positive developments in U.S. trade negotiations. July recorded 113 deals, marking the most active July since at least 2001, excluding the extraordinary issuance years of 2020 and 2021. While August delivered the expected seasonal slowdown with 87 deals, activity accelerated again in September. Following Federal Reserve rate cuts, 118 deals priced during the month, well above the historical September average of 80. In total, 318 deals were completed in the third quarter, making it the most active Q3 on record since at least 2001, again excluding 2020 and 2021.

The market encountered a late-year disruption at the start of the fourth quarter, as a government shutdown delayed the processing of SEC filings. October issuance fell to 86 deals, a 27.1% decline from September. After the government reopened in mid-November, activity resumed, with 98 deals priced in November and 93 in December. Overall, 1,096 equity deals were completed on U.S. exchanges in 2025, ranking it as the fifth most active year for new issuance since at least 2001 and highlighted by a 55% increase in IPO activity.

The IPO Market Reignites

As noted earlier, IPO activity accelerated in 2025, with deal volume rising 55% year-over-year. In total, 349 IPOs—including SPACs—priced during the year, raising $75.5 billion in aggregate proceeds. Excluding the anomaly years of 2020 and 2021, which recorded 458 and 1,009 IPOs, respectively, 2025 marked the most active year for IPO issuance dating back to at least 2001. The previous high occurred in 2007, when 297 companies went public. The increase in issuance was driven in large part by the return of the SPAC IPO. SPAC volumes surged 167% from the prior year and accounted for 41% of total IPOs, the highest share since 2022, when SPACs represented 49% of IPO issuance.

Excluding SPACs, the IPO market remained robust, building on momentum from 2024. Non-SPAC IPO volume increased 20% year-over-year to 205 deals, raising a combined $46.5 billion in proceeds. This represented the strongest year for IPOs since 2014, which saw 284 offerings, again excluding the record-setting years of 2020 and 2021. Proceeds rose 42% from $32.7 billion in 2024, coming just shy of the annual average of $47.0 billion since 2001. Average deal size climbed 19% to $227.0 million, supported by 11 IPOs that exceeded $1 billion in proceeds.

The year’s standout issuer emerged boldly in mid-December from the healthcare sector, as Medline Inc. (MDLN) raised $7.2 billion—the largest IPO since Rivian Automotive, Inc.’s (RIVN) $13.7 billion offering in November 2021. Medline ranked as the ninth-largest IPO and the largest healthcare IPO dating back to at least 2001. Venture Global, Inc. (VG) followed as the second-largest IPO of the year, raising $1.8 billion in January from the energy sector. The technology sector dominated the list of billion-dollar offerings, accounting for eight of the 11 deals above that threshold. This group was led by Klarna Group PLC (KLAR) ($1.6 billion), CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) ($1.6 billion), and Figma, Inc. (FIG) ($1.4 billion).

After leading issuance in 2024, the industrials sector once again finished as the most active, bringing 47 issuers to market and raising $5.9 billion in proceeds, led by BETA Technologies, Inc.’s (BETA) $1.2 billion offering. Technology followed closely, with 43 IPOs raising $15.0 billion—the highest proceeds of any sector. Healthcare rounded out the top three, as 35 issuers raised $11.9 billion.

Looking at pricing performance, IPOs posted an average first-day return of 15.7%, more than double the 7.8% average seen in 2024. Among the 205 non-SPAC IPOs, 13% priced above their initial filing ranges, up slightly from 12% the prior year. Deals that priced above the range significantly outperformed, generating average first-day gains of 44%, compared with returns of 11.4% and 10.1% for deals priced within and below the range, respectively. Waton Financial, Ltd. (WTF) and Figma, Inc. delivered the strongest first-day performances, surging 396.3% and 250.0%. Medline, the year’s largest IPO, posted a first-day return of 41.4%.

Follow-On Issuance Remains Steady

Follow-on offerings remained the most prevalent form of equity issuance in 2025, with 661 transactions pricing and generating $153.6 billion in aggregate proceeds. Issuance volume increased modestly by 5% from the 631 deals completed in 2024, a slower pace than the 21% year-over-year growth recorded from 2023 to 2024. Despite the uptick in deal count, total proceeds declined 2% from $157.1 billion. Average follow-on deal size fell 7% year-over-year to $232.3 million from $249.0 million, even as the number of billion-dollar offerings increased. In total, 36 follow-ons raised more than $1.0 billion in proceeds, a 50% increase from 2024. Follow-on offerings accounted for 60% of overall equity issuance volume in 2025, down from 69% the prior year and slightly below the annual average of 64% since 2001.

The largest follow-on of the year came from Charles Schwab Corp. (SCHW), which raised $13.1 billion in proceeds from the financials sector. This ranked as the eighth-largest follow-on offering dating back to at least 2001. Notably, only 14 follow-on offerings since 2001 have exceeded $10.0 billion in proceeds, 11 of which originated from the financials sector. Brown & Brown, Inc. (BRO), also from the financials sector, recorded the second-largest follow-on of the year, raising $4.4 billion. Rounding out the five largest follow-on offerings of 2025 were Ferrari (RACE), which raised $3.1 billion, and Keurig Dr Pepper, Inc. (KDP), which completed two offerings raising $2.5 billion and $2.4 billion, respectively—all from the consumer goods sector.

As in 2024, healthcare, technology, and industrials dominated follow-on issuance, collectively accounting for 67% of all deals. The healthcare sector led with 280 offerings, surpassing 200 follow-ons for the third consecutive year and extending its position as the most active sector annually since 2011. Technology followed with 94 deals and has recorded the second-highest follow-on activity each year since 2018. Industrials rounded out the top three with 66 offerings.

S&P Global Market Intelligence: Advancing Technology and Execution

The year 2025 proved to be a pivotal one for equity issuance, highlighted by the reacceleration of the IPO market. Looking ahead, the environment appears increasingly supportive for the new issuance market as economic conditions continue to stabilize, inflation trends fall, and the anticipation of lower interest rates. At the same time, the pipeline of private companies preparing for public listings continues to build, led by AI-focused giants such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and Databricks. Several notable technology companies, including SpaceX and Discord, may also emerge as potential public-market entrants.

With that in mind, S&P Global Market Intelligence remains focused on advancing technological innovation, speed of execution, and operational efficiency—reinforcing our commitment to delivering best-in-class solutions for participants across the equity capital markets. Building on the strong foundation established in 2025, we continued to invest in strategic innovation, expand platform capabilities, and deliver client-driven enhancements designed to streamline workflows and improve decision-making.

Throughout the past year, we introduced significant upgrades across our core product suite—including Master Book, Sales Portal, Wall Crossing, and AI & Analytics—unlocking new efficiencies and elevating the client experience. Our teams successfully completed the migration of all users to modern technology, launched the industry’s first multi-bank Sales Portal, and applied artificial intelligence to generate actionable insights at scale. The following highlights showcase key achievements that position S&P Global Market Intelligence for continued growth and leadership in 2026.

Master Book

In 2025, the Equities program completed a major transformation, closing all functional gaps between the legacy Master Book and Accounting Pages, fully migrating clients to the modernized platform. New allocation workflow features, including Fill Down for Allocations and % Fill columns, enhanced flexibility and reduced errors during live bookbuilds. Reporting and compliance were strengthened through Account Coverage Reports, improved FINRA tracking, and the introduction of the Facilitator Dealer role. User experience improvements—such as Multiple Lock Indications and streamlined upload workflows—supported higher-volume transactions while increasing transparency.

Sales Portal

The Sales Portal reached a key milestone with the launch of the Proxy Sales and Syndicate Dashboard, enabling proxy banks to manage equity issuance and distribution across partners from a single, unified dashboard. This innovation reduced operational risk and centralized deal activity for greater efficiency. Enhanced order management—including an updated Add Order panel and right-click edit functionality—made processes faster and more intuitive. Upgrades to investor history and search capabilities, combined with a redesigned interface, delivered a cleaner, more responsive user experience.

Wall Crossing

Enhancements to Wall Crossing in 2025 focused on compliance, communication, and transparency. Integration with client DNS servers enabled seamless and compliant email workflows, while improved recipient management ensured consistency and auditability. The introduction of reusable communication templates and enhanced logging standardized outreach and supported regulatory requirements, ultimately improving the overall user experience.

AI & Analytics

Artificial intelligence became a fully embedded capability across the Equities suite in 2025. The launch of the EBB AI Assistant provided instant, contextual guidance within syndicate pages, boosting efficiency and reducing reliance on manual support. New analytical dashboards offered actionable insights into public and proprietary deal data, empowering teams to make more informed execution decisions.

Looking Ahead: 2026 Roadmap

In 2026, S&P Global Market Intelligence will focus on interoperability, expanded market coverage, and modernizing client experiences. Key initiatives include rebuilding and elevating our analytics suite with enhanced collection, reporting outputs and depth into cross-asset financings, expanding our structured products integration for our retail program, and enhancing Wall Crossing workflows with new visibility and tracking features. The roadmap also addresses EMEA and APAC market requirements while continuing to modernize core platforms—including Equity Bookbuild and the Sales Portal—delivering a seamless, future-ready user experience.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: RXO Is Not Attractive Even Under Optimistic Assumptions On Return On Capital
data.description:
RXO, Inc. (RXO) is a truck freight broker, an 'asset-light' type of business exposed to the trucking cycle, but with different capital requirements (compared to other names I have recently reviewed).

The company is a recent (2022) spin-off from XPO, Inc. (XPO), and therefore its operating history is not very long, but the evidence we have (importantly from the 2020/22 cycle onwards) is not particularly positive in terms of returns on capital. I believe the reason is that RXO participates in a low-barrier-to-entry, low-return, highly commoditized business.

Having this in mind, I cannot understand how the company, which is currently generating operating losses and can cover interest only because of underinvestment, can trade at almost 20x average-cycle earnings or 10x peak-cycle earnings. I don't believe RXO is an opportunity, except for very speculative purposes, and therefore initiate the name as a Hold.

Business Introduction

RXO is a truck freight broker. The company's business is to connect shippers (companies that want to move loads) with carriers (owners and operators of truck fleets to move those loads). RXO may also consolidate billing and payments for shippers and carriers, provide and obtain volume discounts, etc. In general, the company makes a fairly regular gross margin on the dollar volume of freight brokered. In recent years, that number has fallen because of the deep trucking downturn, which squeezes margins across the industry.

In addition to brokerage (70%+ of revenues according to the latest 10-K), the company also does some managed transportation services (a little more white globe service that manages the logistics and freight needs of a client, for 10% of revenues) and last-mile (similar to brokerage, but for short-hauls, 20% of revenues). The company does not disclose these other two businesses as segments, and all is lumped together into a single operating segment on the 10-K, outside of revenues.

Critical Margin Situation

Because of the above-mentioned trucking downcycle, which has lasted for at least 3 years (since early 2023), the company's operating margins have come under significant pressure, breaking the breakeven line this year.

This thin margin is not enough to cover the company's interest expenses on a GAAP basis. The company is not super levered, with $600 million in debts and leases for a company with $1.3 billion in tangible assets, but the margin pressure is high at this time of the cycle.

The company can cover interest and maintain a relatively breakeven FCF because it has been cutting CAPEX significantly (as compared to D&A below). Part of D&A includes amortization of intangibles from acquisitions for about $46 million per year (see the 3Q25 earnings release for 9M25 figures, annualized). These kinds of intangibles do not really deplete, so the business should not be damaged by lower investment. Still, without margin improvements, the company will find itself in more cash flow challenges.

Low Returns on Capital

Although RXO's debts are inherited from the time of the spin-off, I do not know if the business should have any debt at all. Its returns on capital are low, especially during cyclical downturns, and therefore, interest can become a burden.

Calculating the capital returns for RXO is not trivial. The company calls itself 'asset-light' because it does not need trucks, but when we consider its operating income over assets (my preferred measure of return), we find that its capital return is relatively low (6% pre-tax below or ~4.5% post-tax).

However, some might argue that total assets is not representative of the company's capital needs because it includes intangibles and goodwill (about 50% of the balance sheet historically), coming from the spin-off and from acquisitions (like last year's acquisition of UPS' UK brokerage business, Coyote). If we removed these, then the return on (tangible) assets would be at least 2x, or about 12% on average—not that bad.

I can agree with that view on a go-forward, no-further-acquisition basis. If the company froze its acquisitions today, it is possible that its businesses would return closer to 12% tangible assets. However, if the company uses capital (equity, debt, or earnings) to acquire businesses and pays a premium on those businesses, then it will keep a low return on invested capital.

Without the acquisition of Coyote in 2024 (a press release indicates the business generated about $3.2 billion in revenues in 2023), the company (only $2 billion above its 2023 revenues) would not have grown at all and even potentially shrunk.

Valuation Under Both Capital Scenarios

In order to value the company, we can work with both capital scenarios, because they yield the same result today and historically, albeit not the same going forward.

Because using total assets (including intangibles and goodwill) or using tangible assets modifies the denominator, the higher return on assets is offset by a lower asset base. Using a 6% average return on a total asset base of $3 billion is the same as using a 12% average return on a tangible asset base of $1.5 billion; both indicate an average operating income of $180 million. After interest of about $35 million and taxes of 25%, we get to $110 million in net income. Compared to a market cap of $2.42 billion, this already does not look great (an earnings yield of only 4.5%). Even if we add back the non-cash amortization expense for $46 million, we get $156 million in cash-distributable income, or a yield of 6.5%, below the market.

Only if we adopted extreme optimism and based our calculations on 2022 cycle-peak margins (11% over the total asset base) would we get a moderate 11% yield ($3 billion in assets results in $330 million in operating income, which, after taxes and interest, becomes ~$220 million, to which we add $45 million in non-cash intangibles amortization).

That is, without growth, under regular assumptions (still optimistic because they assume a return to the median in the cycle), we get a 6.5% distributable yield, and only after high optimism assumptions, a 10%+ yield. This is for a company that today can barely cover interest and that I believe has very little in terms of competitive advantages.

In order to add growth, the picture gets more complicated. The company has not grown organically since the spin-off, and inorganic growth requires paying premiums that become intangibles (the Coyote acquisition was almost purely intangibles, according to the 10-K).

Assuming constant returns (over total assets in this case), in order to grow 5%, the company would expand its balance sheet (mainly intangibles via acquisitions, plus some receivables) at 5% per year. However, because the post-tax returns on assets are less than 5% (4.5% as expressed above), the company cannot self-finance that 5% of growth. It has to either issue equity, as it did last year, diluting its shareholders, or issue debt, which is risky.

If the company used all of the $156 million in distributable cash as calculated above, that would basically yield about 5% growth in the balance sheet, but at the expense of receiving no other source of return.

That is, at current prices, even assuming a return to the mean, RXO only promises an MSD kind of return, either from earnings without growth, or from growth (without distributable earnings). I think this is an extremely low return for a stock that is risky if the cycle doesn't turn and that earns a subpar return on total capital.

For these reasons, I initiate RXO with a Hold rating.

data.title: Sanlorenzo: Luxury Positioning With Strong Visibility
data.description:
For the first time, we moved on to analysing a yacht manufacturer. While this may appear unconventional for our readers, it is a natural extension of our long-standing focus on luxury goods. Sanlorenzo (SNLRF) is a well-established name recognized for the quality of its yachts and the strength of its leadership, particularly its CEO.

Back to basics, given it's our initiation of coverage, we start with the company profile. Sanlorenzo is an Italian shipbuilder focused on the design, production, and commercialisation of bespoke luxury yachts. Taking advantage of the H1 results presentation, the company is divided into four business units across three distinct brands (Fig. 1): 1 Sanlorenzo brand covers yachts and 2) superyachts, 3) Bluegame (sport utility yachts), and 4) Nautor Swan (sailing yachts).

Source: Sanlorenzo H1 results ppt - Fig 1

Why are we Positive?

Sanlorenzo published its results in mid-November 2025, and much of the disclosed information is already reflected in market expectations. As customary, the analysis looks ahead, with the following sections outlining the key forward-looking highlights:

The group is a long-established Italian yacht builder, drawing on 67 years of craftsmanship in the luxury segment. In aggregate, Sanlorenzo generates €930 million in top-line from new yacht deliveries within a global boating market of roughly €35 billion (source: Italian Marine Industry Association and Deloitte). According to the report, Italy remains the world leader in Superyacht production with a 54% market share. The company’s “made-to-measure” model is highly customized and combines standardized exterior platforms. This has scale benefits and supports cost optimisation while reducing execution risk. Here at the Lab, we particularly appreciate its positioning in the 30–50 meter range. Indeed, yachts are well-suited to Mediterranean cruising while still capable of transatlantic use, including the Caribbean. Aside from physical limits on the yacht's length, a few regions are starting to restrict where vessels can operate. So, we believe there is less strategic appeal in the >100 meters yacht market.

Q3 2025 results were mixed. Top-line sales came in short of Wall Street expectations, and management attributed it to higher demand in the larger 30m segment (Fig. 2). This will require longer build times and will delay P&L sales. Based on our intelligence, this is not a negative structural signal, and we believe Sanlorenzo's mid-term growth profile remains attractive.

Source: Sanlorenzo Q3 results ppt - Fig 2

The company's Q3 order book showed renewed momentum. The company recorded year-to-date order book growth of 18.4% (Fig. 3). The backlog provides meaningful visibility and is a clear positive sign in our investment case. We should report that Sanlorenzo’s products and innovations have been a key contributor to stronger order intake. This will likely support both its earnings growth story.

Fig 3

Key to the report is that scarcity and direct distribution remain central to Sanlorenzo's equity story. The Sanlorenzo standalone brand accounted for about 80% of the company's totalsales in 2024. The company sold 69 new yachts. Roughly half of the group's top-line sales come from repeat customers, who tend to upgrade every 4–5 years. This provides a key upside to us. Additionally, 76% of these clients decided to increase their expenses compared to their prior purchase. This implies earnings visibility and lower intermediary costs.

We also see Sanlorenzo distribution as a key differentiator. We like the company's capital strategic priorities. The latest acquisition of Simpson Marine (adding 14 direct points of presence in APAC) and the build-out of the U.S. footprint, supported by the American Magic partnership, should accelerate growth in both regions. The Americas are expected to account for around 21% of sales in 2025, up from 16% in 2024.

Lastly, Sanlorenzo's valuation appears undemanding at this stage. The company's shares trade at approximately a 10x P/E on 2026 estiamtes. While a double-digit P/E is not cheap, we believe Sanlorenzo is more of a luxury peerthan an industrial player. Valuation is 5x lower than its long-term average, despite a 6% EPS CAGR. Therefore, we see this as an attractive entry point. Additionally, the company paid a €1 dividend, which may grow this year (yield 3.3%), and there is an ongoing buyback, further underpinning our investment case.

Earnings and Valuation

We expect Sanlorenzo to continue expanding in sales in 2026 (Fig. 4). This is supported by the company's backlog and its M&A activities. Going down to the P&L, we see EBIT growth both in absolute terms and on a percentage basis. With the new product MIX (New Swan Alloy), we believe Sanlorenzo might target€1 billion in sales (a 4% top-line sales growth vs. 2025 numbers). Even applying the same EBIT margin (14.6%), Sanlorenzo can achieve a €146 million in core operating profit. With a 30% tax rate, our 2026 net profit estimate is €102.2 million. This implied an EPS of €2.87 and a P/E of 10.5x. This does not include: more deliveries, a higher EBIT margin, potential positive interest in 2026 given FCF evolution and no debt (Fig. 5), and no lower outstanding shares. In the Q3 call, management noted that net debt is closely linked to NWC trends and that by year-end, they expect a favorable net financial position.

Going to the valuation, Sanlorenzo's historical P/E has been above 15x. At this stage, the company trades at a 2026 P/E of 10.5x and an EV/EBITDA of 5.5x. This is not representative of our luxury coverage. Assigning a valuation multiple is invariably challenging, as it requires balancing forward expectations with inherently uncertain assumptions. So, we use a blend valuation methodology with a target multiple of 7.5x on the EBITDA and a P/E of 12x. We derive a target price of €39 per share, representing a 25% upside from the current level.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Risks

Sanlorenzo downside risks include macroeconomic and geopolitical volatility; however, we see limited disruption in sales. The company might be exposed to potential shifts in consumer preferences; however, these products are tailor-made. Sanlorenzo is exposed to supply-chain interruptions due to its significant reliance on external suppliers, capacity bottlenecks, and additional ERG requirements. Sanlorenzo risks include higher competitive pressure in a relatively concentrated market and execution risk on the latest M&A deal.

Conclusion

We like the sector and the Sanlorenzo subsegment within the industry. The company valuation is not demanding, and we have not included potential upside in our investment case. Therefore, we initiate coverage of Sanlorenzo with a clear buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/15/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: EOG Resources: Building Out Natural Gas Production Will Help It Power Data Centers
data.description:
I last wrote about EOG Resources (EOG) in February of 2025. In that article I basically pointed out that EOG Resources was a safe, yet boring investment. They have a good dividend yield, a very solid balance sheet, and have just been profitably expanding for decades. They have not been a fast grower and, quite frankly, do little to excite investors, as you aren't likely to get 100%+ returns in the next year investing in EOG, like you might if you pick the right AI-related names.

I still think EOG is the perfect place to park unused capital, especially if you find most of your capital concentrated on AI names or the Mag7 (so that would also include if the majority of your money is invested in the S&P or Nasdaq). EOG has a near 4% dividend that has been growing at a CAGR around 20% for the last 5 years, so they comfortably beat even elevated inflation we've been seeing. And although, as I mentioned above, you may get 100%+ returns if you pick the right AI names, you can also lose 80-100% of your investment if you pick the wrong ones. That just isn't the case for EOG. Because EOG is in a commodity industry, they do still see big drawdowns, but those are always temporary, and they always bounce back. You can keep collecting the dividends while you wait.

EOG has been underperforming the market by a wide margin, with terrible 2025 returns, but it's not the short-term share price movements that you care about if you buy into EOG. To me, it is the place to park capital if you find many other parts of the market ahead of themselves but don't want to have too much capital in cash due to inflation.

I personally still think the Mag 7 will do very well over the next 5 years or 10 years. Maybe not all of them, but most of them, due to the ways they'll be able to save money from AI and get extra synergies that smaller companies can't manage. I also think there will be many AI names that will win over the next decade, and returns on them will be fantastic; picking the right ones will be a lot harder. However, I am definitely not putting all my capital in the tech or the Mag 7, in case there is an AI bubble and it gets popped in the coming years. Also, I think the Mag 7 could have a tough next 1-3 years, even if they do well over the next 5-10, which is another reason I'm moving more of my money into other names in the short term.

Diversification and the Next Chapter in the AI Story

It's always important to stay diversified if you want to protect your capital. Being 100% invested in AI names would have worked exceedingly well over the last few years, but the party always comes to a stop eventually. I'm sure people justified staying invested in tech names before the dot-com bubble, but we all know how much those names suffered for many years after that. I'm not saying AI is 100% akin to the dot-com bubble, but there are some similarities.

Even if the big tech companies continue to keep pouring CAPEX money into expanding their AI capabilities for the foreseeable future, I expect the companies that win from that will start changing. NVIDIA (NVDA) was, of course, the big winner for a couple of years, along with many others such as Alphabet (GOOG), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Nebius (NBIS) really taking off in 2025 as other big winners have started to become more clear.

Basically, for the most part, the winners have been chip/graphics card makers, hyperscalers, and those building out servers/data centers. There have been some energy-related names that have started to be winners from AI, such as Bloom Energy (BE), as they have made deals to help supply clean energy for data centers. At some point I expect this will spread to all energy names that provide any sort of energy for data centers. Oil may not be as important for this, but natural gas is still very important to powering the infrastructure that is being built out to serve AI needs.

And that's where I get back to EOG. They are still primarily an oil company, but they also produce a large amount of natural gas, and they have been looking to expand their natural gas production. In just the last year, they have increased their natural gas production from 1,970 MMcfd in Q3 2024 to 2,745 MMcfd in Q3 2025, good for almost a 40% increase Y/Y. With their natural gas growing at a much faster pace these last few years than their oil production, this has put their mix right around 70% oil and 30% natural gas in the most recent quarter. This strategy seems to be in line with the fact that the United States is undergoing some amount of electrification, lessening the need for oil, but building out data centers at a very rapid rate, increasing the need for natural gas and other cleaner forms of energy.

Great Entry Point

From a technical perspective, I like EOG a lot too. I look at technical analysis as secondary to fundamental analysis, but if I like a company's fundamentals, I think taking a technical look can offer some extra info into when the best entry points may be. Here is a look at EOG over the last 5 years:

EOG has had a pretty hard floor of $100 over the last 4 years. Technically, if they break under this, they could go lower, but when I'm just buying for the dividend and holding for the long term, I'm just looking for good entry points. I consider this one of them. Either they hit their floor of the last few years, they go higher, and I lock in at one of the higher dividend yields I could grab, or they go lower and I wait for the next good entry point to buy more. I am still leaving some money on the side and not making this quite a "full" position. This is also how they have looked over the last few decades:

Outside of the pandemic that sent oil prices briefly negative because of how much supply chains and general world transportation were disrupted, EOG has just kept marching higher for decades. I think this is from their approach of continual, slow, profitable growth. Some oil and gas companies take on tons of debt to make acquisitions and try to grow as much as they can during boom times.

I see EOG as a company that always makes the fiscally responsible decisions and never takes on much risk. This is kind of ironic, since EOG (which stands for Enron Oil and Gas) Resources was spun off (in 1999) from the Enron that was a part of so many scandals in 2001. EOG Resources was never a part of the scandals. I just thought it was a stark contrast between EOG Resources, which has always been fiscally responsible, and their former parent company, Enron, which was involved in the big accounting scandals, but I digress.

To show just how fiscally responsible EOG has been over the years, here are their debt-to-equity and debt-to-EBITDA ratios compared to some of their peers:

EOG has still taken on debt when they've needed to or they've deemed it a good growth opportunity that offers high enough returns. But they have been very conservative, especially over the last 10 years, which really showed its importance during Covid, as the other oil producers debt levels shot much higher.

Where is Oil Going?

I have seen arguments for oil prices going up and for them going down. On the one hand, drilling of shale formations, fracking, and other technological advances have opened up many more ways to keep extracting oil without expensive new megaprojects and exploration needed. This has become apparent as the US has become the world's leading oil producer in recent years, as its output has surged for more than a decade. Add the issue of electrification driving down the need for as much oil in the future, as well as the fact that OPEC has already been holding back considerable capacity, and oil bear arguments have some real merit.

On the other side, they would argue that there has been underinvestment in oil for many years and that eventually all the cheap, easy-to-access spots unlocked by new technologies will have been used up. For now and likely for many decades, the world is still very dependent on oil. Some of the most developed nations are working to be less reliant on it, but for now, oil is still a very inelastic resource. The price has to go up a lot before people stop buying as much of it, since it is generally used to commute to jobs and provide the main way to live lives the way people are used to.

To get to the point of this section, I don't know at all where oil prices are going. I tend to think they could still go down further in the short term. There does seem to be a supply glut that OPEC can't just make up for by taking some oil offline, as they already have quite a bit offline. The United States, Canada, and some other non-OPEC nations have expanded their production so much in the last decade or two that it could still take a while to even out. However, the longer the price of oil stays low or goes lower, the more I think it will jump up in the future. And EOG still has enough natural gas production to keep the next few years looking bright.

Conclusion

It doesn't matter too much where oil goes in the short term, because EOG offers an investment that has very good dividends through whatever happens in the short term and a real option-like return possible if oil ever jumps up in the medium or long term. The longer oil stays at a reasonable range and oil E&P companies have subpar returns, the more I think they will pay off if and when demand ever outstrips supply for a longer period of time.

EOG is a company with a stellar balance sheet, a very long history through all sorts of oil environments, and great margins that should help it stay profitable even if oil prices fall from their current levels. They are one of my top picks in the sector to park money for steadying dividends and a possible big upside if held for the years and decades ahead. I think oil will eventually surge at some point over the next decade, but until that time, EOG is focusing on increasing their natural gas production, which should do well for the next few years.

data.title: TBG: Why I'm Not Sold On This High-Conviction Dividend ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article continues my coverage of the TBG Dividend Focus ETF (TBG), last reviewed six months ago. In this update, I will reiterate that TBG offers few meaningful advantages over a similar-yielding passive fund like the Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV). Although I am interested in seeing how TBG's managers adapt over a full market cycle, I recommend interested investors take a "watch and see" approach for now. Therefore, I've rated it a "hold" today, with plans to downgrade it in 2026 should its fundamentals and relative performance against FDVV not improve.

TBG Overview

TBG is an actively managed fund comprised of 30-40 stocks with a record of consistently increasing their dividends. The fund manager's active approach uses quantitative and qualitative measures to assess dividend sustainability and growth potential. Underpinning TBG's strategy is the belief that dividend growth and quality are strongly connected, and if a company can increase its dividends by more than 5% per year over a 5-7 year market cycle, there's a good chance it's on solid financial footing.

TBG's expense ratio is high at 0.59%, as is its 0.09% 30-day median bid/ask spread. As of January 13, 2026, TBG's 30-day SEC yield is 3.26%, and the fund makes distributions quarterly, with its most recent dividend including a $0.0679 per share capital gain. REITs are eligible holdings, so not all of TBG's distributions are qualified.

TBG Dividends and Performance

TBG shareholders saw a 25% increase in dividends last year. However, most was manufactured growth. New ETFs often have volatile dividend growth rates, and large percentage changes in shares outstanding can distort per-share payments. This is evident because TBG's current portfolio is three-quarters similar to last year, and the common holdings only increased their dividends by 7% in aggregate last year. That's in line with the manager's stated 5-7% objective, but I don't want readers to get the impression that 25% annual dividend growth is sustainable. It almost certainly is not, absent deliberate and regular rebalances to higher-yielding securities.

TBG's performance track record is short, but between December 2023 and December 2025, it managed to outperform the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) by an annualized 5.32% (16.13% vs. 10.81%). TBG also lagged behind FDVV by 5.41% with more volatility, leading to inferior risk-adjusted and downside risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino Ratios).

It's no coincidence that FDVV was the better performer over this bullish period, since it's more growth-oriented than TBG and SCHD. FDVV uses what I call a "barbell" approach to dividend investing, which involves selecting high- and low-yielding stocks that are loosely correlated. This approach allows for a respectable 2.77% estimated dividend yield even though Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL) are its top two holdings. Meanwhile, TBG and SCHD hold stocks with roughly the same dividend yields, but the key difference is TBG's 0.59% expense ratio. This high fee reduces TBG's expected dividend yield to just 3.38%, or only 0.61% more than FDVV's. In my view, this is not enough extra income to matter.

TBG Sector Allocations vs. FDVV and SCHD

As of January 10, 2026, TBG had 18% and 14% allocated to the Health Care and Energy sectors. Compared to SCHD and FDVV, TBG is underweight Technology by 8% on average, and overall, it's very well diversified at the sector level. Still, I'm somewhat surprised, given how TBG markets itself as an active fund applying a "bottom-up and sector-agnostic" strategy. With turnover rates of only 8% and 26% the last two years, it seems that TBG has settled into a well-diversified portfolio it intends to mostly keep moving forward. Therefore, intentional or not, I believe diversification is key to the strategy.

TBG Fundamentals vs. FDVV: More Weaknesses Than Strengths

In my thesis, I mentioned how TBG holds few meaningful advantages over FDVV from a fundamentals perspective. Broadly, here is how I have the two funds ranked on five key fundamental factors:

This table indicates that TBG is less risky and has more value factor exposure than FDVV. In contrast, TBG's 2.16/10 growth score is substantially lower than FDVV's 4.84/10 score, and it's also slightly deficient on quality and momentum at the moment. Given how we know growth and momentum stocks have led markets over the last few years, these scores align with the performance differences we saw earlier.

The underlying statistics for each fund support this conclusion. On a weighted average basis, TBG's components have sales and earnings growth rates that are roughly half those of FDVV's. Given the fund manager's 5-7% dividend growth goal over a full cycle, TBG's 4.34% three-year EPS CAGR is slightly low, and this will need to improve to avoid stretching the payout ratio to uncomfortable levels. Already, TBG's 65.50% ratio is high, and FDVV appears to have more leeway to increase dividends due to its lower payout ratio and higher earnings growth rates.

TBG and FDVV have comparable weighted free cash flow margins of around 14%, but from an operations and capital efficiency perspective, FDVV's EBIT margins and ROTC ratio are materially better.

TBG's growth and quality gaps relative to FDVV have more to do with what it underweights rather than what it overweights. As shown below, TBG has zero exposure to ten stocks that, in aggregate, increased their earnings per share by an annualized 17% over the last three years, have a payout ratio of just 25%, and have free cash flow margins of 25%. These stocks comprise 34% of FDVV, and while it's true that they only yield 1.06% on average, it's a massive fundamental tradeoff for only a marginal 0.61% net improvement in yield.

Risks to Thesis

TBG and FDVV are subject to change, so these fundamental differences might not hold moving forward. TBG also currently has advantages over FDVV on value and risk, which could prove beneficial if market enthusiasm declines in 2026. To illustrate how, consider statistics like TBG's 0.76 five-year portfolio beta and 18.91x trailing P/E ratio, which suggest it's less risky and more value-oriented than FDVV. Rather than having Nvidia and Apple on its top ten holdings list, TBG holds several defensive Health Care names like Merck & Co. (MRK) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ).

To assess the likelihood of a market slowdown, I track S&P 500 quarterly earnings surprises. Historically, decelerating or below-average surprises often precede valuation re-ratings or market crashes, with the latest example being in mid/late 2021. As shown below, earnings surprises began to decelerate from their post-pandemic highs, and ultimately, the index's TTM P/E declined by 15% in 2022, per this S&P report.

The Technology sector's P/E compressed further by 26% that year, so given FDVV's +17% overweight in that sector, I can see how negative sentiment could lead to TBG outperforming. However, with surprises consistently above average, it's too early to make that call, so it's not my base case.

Investment Recommendation

I reiterate my view that FDVV is superior to TBG for dividend investors. Its 2.77% expected yield is only 0.61% lower than TBG's, and this difference is more than offset by material advantages in growth and quality. TBG's 0.59% expense ratio is another important headwind that negatively impacts total returns and distributions, so while today's "hold" rating reflects my patience for seeing how the active fund can adapt to changing market conditions, I anticipate downgrading TBG to a "sell" sometime in 2026 if its fundamentals and performance do not improve.
